body: What is your use case for this method in a bounding volume class?
author: Mugen87

body:
I am using `Box2` class for some 2D tasks on a canvas. `Box2` is useful as a kind of "rectangle" that I need to manipulate in several ways, e.g. create the box from a array of points (`Vector2`), inflate (`expandByPoint`, `expandByScalar`), translate, etc.

This time I just need to draw (stroke) a rectangle on a canvas using the `Box2` coordinates but before that I have to do some raw pixel manipulation (using `getImageData`) in the same area where rectangle will be drawn so I need to start the pixel manipulation on the same top-left point where the rectangle will be drawn exactly. In order to prevent some different starting point between these two task I prefer to round `Box2` before. It's just an example, there are many other tasks where box's round() would be useful.

I thought round() method on Box2/3 wouldn't be problematic considering Vector2/3 can already be rounded too.
author: delmohf

body:
> I thought round() method on Box2/3 wouldn't be problematic considering Vector2/3 can already be rounded too.

I understand your use case but adding `round()` to bounding volumes feels not right. It's a matter of semantics. 

Normally you do not "round" an AABB. It's okay to round raw numbers or certain math primitives like a vector but certainly not matrices, planes, rays....or bounding volumes.

author: mrdoob

body:
> I did run into [.WebGL-0x1ec401258700] GL_INVALID_OPERATION: Texture is immutable. under certain circumstances,

This warning is related to the `texStorage()` API. It usually appears when a texture is defined with a certain dimension via `texStorage2D()` and later updated with a larger dimension via `texSubImage2D()` (which is not valid). 

It's the responsibility of the app that a textures dimensions does not change after its initial use.

>  Reflector will now no longer work in WebGL 1 (unless the user specifies multisample: 1) and it will log a warning in the console right?

Yes. However, the user has to specify a `multisample` of `0` to deactivate multisampling. And this already happens without the PR.
author: Mugen87
Mugen87

body:
>>  Reflector will now no longer work in WebGL 1 (unless the user specifies multisample: 1) and it will log a warning in the console right?
>
> Yes. However, the user has to specify a `multisample` of `0` to deactivate multisampling. And this already happens without the PR.

I think we should follow what we do with `texture.anisotropy` where the user can set it to `16` and the renderer will do as much as the GPU supports.

So if the user sets `rendertarget.samples` to `4` the renderer will try to use multisample, otherwise it will render without and it'll look aliased.

That way the user doesn't have to check for support and things will continue working.

body: I don't think we should add these methods to `Euler`. If users want to interpolate rotation values, they should work with `Quaternion`.
author: Mugen87

body: Allocating a new `Quaternion` object and passing data to it performing slerp and then back to the `Euler` seems like a lot of steps to achieve something that's mostly just a simple division. When looking for an answer on stackoverflow and tutorials almost no one does it this way. They just suggest to do it manually or use [third party library](https://javascript.tutorialink.com/three-js-how-to-slowly-rotate-a-mesh-until-it-reaches-a-specific-value/)
author: pacyfist

body:
> When looking for an answer on stackoverflow and tutorials almost no one does it this way.

I don't think this statement is true. Furthermore, I can not approve the resource you have linked. This is not the way you should do it.

I suggest you read `3D Math Primer for Graphics and Game Development`, section `8.3.4 Disadvantages of Euler Angles` to understand why interpolating between two orientations with euler angles is problematic. The conclusion is to always use quaternions. 

If you want to transition from a source orientation to a target orientation, use `Quaternion.rotateTowards()`. It is essentially the same as Unity's [Quaternion.RotateTowards()](https://docs.unity3d.com/ScriptReference/Quaternion.RotateTowards.html) (which has no euler class at all btw).

Now I'm realizing we dealt with this years ago already in #17855.

@JordyvanDortmont said this:

> Nvidia's default settings enable anisotropic filtering in the control panel for the GTX 1070 and GTX 2060 (Mobile). When you disable anisotropic filtering for Chrome and Firefox, the seams are gone. This is unfortunate, because most users won't disable anisotropic filtering manually, so they'll see the seams.

And @sciecode said this:

>> To avoid cube map seams, I create an extra pixel around each face. This way when the cube map is sampled by an application later(with a little care by sampling the centre of the texel), the extra 1 border of pixels makes sure that there is no seams artifacts present.
>
> Clearly the creators knew about the possibility of cube map seams and devised a solution to it, but perhaps they failed to considered that anisotropic wouldn't guarantee sampling from the center of the texel.

author: AleksandarCebov

body:
Great work, and thanks @Mugen87 and @AleksandarCebov!

Thoughts, in no particular order ...

- Morphing vertex colors and UVs seems like a very useful technique, and _with a good authoring workflow_ I expect artists and devs will do interesting things with it.
- Popular workflows do not support the technique today. I checked Blender, Maya, and Unreal Engine, and all appear to support only positions and normals in morph targets.
- If we were to pick only one of the two (morph colors vs morph UVs) I might choose UVs, since that provides the option of morphing colors on a simple gradient, or more complex animations like flipboards or scrolling textures. Of course we could also do both.
- Node-based shaders can give similar results without the need of a dedicated morph target feature within the renderer. Usability might be a bit lower, e.g. users must identify the right input node to morph the colors, rather than accessing `mesh.morphTargetInfluences`. And I'm not sure of the right timeline for providing node-based materials from GLTFLoader.


tl;dr — We'd be stepping out a bit "in front" of the ecosystem by adding this feature before real workflows exist. There are times when that's a good thing to do, so I'm not opposed to adding this, but just want to be sure we're aware of both the implications and alternatives.

Didn't know ro.me used this method! Do you recall what the Blender workflow was? I guess it was using three.js' custom Blender exporter at that point...

I could hook something up with glTF Transform to support this, using steps like...

1. Create 2+ sets of vertex colors in Blender, optionally include some (position-only) morph targets, export to glTF/GLB
2. Run CLI command to associate vertex colors with morph targets, where COLOR_0 is the base:
  ```
    gltf-transform morphAttributes input.glb output.glb --assign COLOR_1:MorphName1,COLOR_2:MorphName2
  ```
3. Import with THREE.GLTFLoader

body:
Why `slice()` instead of `substring()`?
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/String/substring
author: mrdoob

body:
Both have their use cases. Both return the same in most cases but can [differ](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/String/substring#differences_between_substring_and_slice) if the second parameter is smaller than the first or negative.
I personally like that `slice()` has less "magic" and is more likely to act the way you want to (or think it does). For example in cases where you expect the second parameter to be possibly negative and you want to replicate `substr()` you would use `substring()` other than that I like to use `slice()`
author: CommanderRoot

body: Seems like `substring()` is more strict?
author: mrdoob

body: I would say the opposite due to the parameter switching `substring()` can do but I guess it really depends on how you plan to provide the input variables to these functions.
author: CommanderRoot

body:
> For example in cases where you expect the second parameter to be possibly negative and you want to replicate `substr()` you would use `substring()`

Hmm.... Is that why you're using `substring()` in `ColladaLoader.js` and `webgl_geometry_text.html`?
author: mrdoob

body: That's correct. In both cases the first parameter is >= 0. If the second one would be negative `substring()` will return an empty string (like `substr()`), `slice()` however would return everything from the start until the last x chars

author: LeviPesin

body:
This PR adds a grand total of 2 lines and changes a few in an example file that many, many people use and that is over 1200 lines long because it tries to (and succeeds, for the most part!) to be _the_ example for how to do OrbitControls. So much, that for almost every project it stays the right solution.
Here's the full diff for comparison: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23526/files.

There were changes based on what was requested, the question of "do other people need this" was answered positively with a good amount of users chiming in and referencing issues and PRs.

A clear usecase was given, loading a glTF file with a camera and attaching OrbitControls to that, which is currently broken. cc @donmccurdy 

A known existing bug (that was documented, turning it into a somehow "intentional limitation") is fixed by this PR.

What else is needed to see something as improvement to three.js? This is a serious question. I'd love to learn more about this - we were planning to contribute bug fixes and improvements back to three.js, but the experience with this PR here doesn't make that feel viable anymore. cc @mrdoob

I'm also now really confused about "core" vs. "examples" in regards to three; my understanding was that "core" needs to be polished, small, strict, and examples have a bit more leeway when it comes to implementation details. The history of this PR suggests otherwise though.

**TL;DR:** I find it really strange that a small bugfix like this can't be merged for confusing reasons.
author: hybridherbst

body:
I understand how this looks like simply a small bugfix, but it's also an increase in the scope of OrbitControls — and the PR only partially implements that additional scope (to confirm: are rotated/scaled ancestors fully supported?), likely leaving three.js maintainers to deal with the bug reports and possibly implementation of the rest. If the rest of that scope is similarly trivial then (for myself at least) I wouldn't be opposed here.

> > I am not in favor of this PR. Use another controls, or hack your own copy. That is why the controls are part of the examples -- and not in core.
> 
> That's not how people use examples -- they import them alongside three. It's confusing to see the disparity in focus here and with loaders, for example. Are we misusing these modules? What's the policy in usage/modification to these?

It is hard to answer "what are the examples" in any universally coherent way. Some are basic, supporting common use cases and intentionally simple enough for end-user customization (e.g. most controls and helpers). Some are complex, aiming to be _the_ solution for a problem, and more challenging to customize (e.g. loaders). Other modules are drifting somewhere in the middle, generally increasing in complexity and maintenance cost over time.

There are good reasons why projects like [three-gpu-pathtracer](https://github.com/gkjohnson/three-gpu-pathtracer/) or [three-camera-controls](https://github.com/yomotsu/camera-controls) or [three-pathfinding](https://github.com/donmccurdy/three-pathfinding) are not part of this repository. At a certain level of complexity it would be too much burden for core maintainers, or — if there is a dedicated maintainer — they decide that maintenance in a dedicated repository is easier, given full freedom to control the build process, unit tests, type-checking, dependencies, and so on.

I think the conflict here is the tension of trying to keep a popular example like OrbitControls from drifting slowly into the latter category. Sorry for the confusion in the grey area. 😔
author: donmccurdy

body:
> likely leaving three.js maintainers to deal with the bug reports and possibly implementation of the rest

I'd prefer if something like this was stated instead if implicit, because then there could be a discussion what that rest is in this particular case. In other areas of three (e.g. the loaders) the approach seems to improve piece by piece, instead of covering all known and unknown usecases. I very much understand there needs to be a line between what goes in a project and lives elsewhere. The OrbitControls do live in three.js though.

Regarding rotated/scaled parents: my understanding is that this PR ensures the Orbit Controls actually do what the name implies - orbiting around the orbit target. The only thing it changes is that this works, even when the camera doing the orbit is parented to something. It does not change any behaviour in regards to how orbit radius, distance, etc. are applied.
author: hybridherbst

body:
@marwie, @hybridherbst and others who feel frustrated by the reasoning behind the rejection of this PR, are you aware of the [three-stdlib](https://github.com/pmndrs/three-stdlib) fork? Or partial fork I guess since it's just the examples folder. 

I believe it was created because many PRs for three.js examples are rejected in a similar manner to this one. You may have more luck submitting new features there. 

For the actual reasoning, which I believe can be stated as: _limit the scope of the examples and keep them as **examples** rather than **production grade plugins**, to reduce the burden on three.js maintainers and keep the focus of this project on improving the core and building support for important features like WebGPU_ - I do think it's a valid reason. You're looking at it from a technical perspective and feeling frustration that a simple fix is rejected (and I have felt this before too). Long time maintainers like @Mugen87 and @donmccurdy are looking at it with the experience of being involved in the day to day running of this repo for many years and understanding that there's only so much that can be done in one unfunded open source project. From this perspective it's important to keep a tight rein on scope creep.

Both are valid perspectives but running this project in a way that maintains its focus has to take priority. If your PR gets rejected with this reasoning, and you still think your code is important enough to share, you should host it elsewhere, like the stdlib repo or in this case see if @yomotsu's camera-controls already supports this. 

As for the responsibility of the maintainers here, I think it's important to acknowledge that this issue does keep coming up and causing frustration for contributors because, as @donmccurdy says, what will or won't be accepted is often a "grey area".  What can we do to communicate this better and make it easier for new contributors to feel welcome here?

body:
> @marwie, @hybridherbst and others who feel frustrated by the reasoning behind the rejection of this PR, are you aware of the [three-stdlib](https://github.com/pmndrs/three-stdlib) fork? Or partial fork I guess since it's just the examples folder.

FWIW, that project first existed because of friction with using examples in modern toolchains (blaming Node CJS/ESM here) and ongoing issues with tree-shaking. I've been looking into backporting fixes here, but aside from tree-shaking, I don't believe it's possible without a build step for examples (and that's already an area I don't want to touch with services like Skypack failing). I did try to suggest similar for three addons for a three-styled bundle, but I realize this is a bit offtopic.

> I believe it was created because many PRs for three.js examples are rejected in a similar manner to this one. You may have more luck submitting new features there.

Notably, three-stdlib had module conversions to Typescript, but it's 1-1 with examples' features (or behind) except exactly OrbitControls with side-effects removed from all controls (see #20575). I believe these frustrations are almost exclusive to controls, in the case of side-effects and general design for OrbitControls to expose internal methods and (seemingly) bug fixes like this one. Part of this, I think, is because of how widely used OrbitControls is, which is why my immediate comparison is GLTFLoader, but I don't know how easy that is for people to contribute to or modify for their own purposes to make a good comparison.

> For the actual reasoning, which I believe can be stated as: limit the scope of the examples and keep them as examples rather than production grade plugins, to reduce the burden on three.js maintainers and keep the focus of this project on improving the core and building support for important features like WebGPU - I do think it's a valid reason. You're looking at it from a technical perspective and feeling frustration that a simple fix is rejected (and I have felt this before too)

I don't like how inconsistent this is because I could go from touching what would otherwise be educational material to something that extends the core and is held to a much higher standard. I hinted at this above as possible misuse, but people use these modules as-is in production and come here if they see a wide enough issue/use-case to fix or support. I think there's a larger organizational issue at hand here or at least something to consider or clarify regarding the intent/organization of the current examples (in regards to scope as per your comment) or general project (with how this is laid out on Github with access to https://github.com/threejs). I wonder how much overhead maintaining these examples in lockstep with the core adds, but I understand the decision to keep distribution unified.

> As for the responsibility of the maintainers here, I think it's important to acknowledge that this issue does keep coming up and causing frustration for contributors because, as @donmccurdy says, what will or won't be accepted is often a "grey area". What can we do to communicate this better and make it easier for new contributors to feel welcome here?

I have no issue accepting this in [three-stdlib](https://github.com/pmndrs/three-stdlib), but I'm hesitant to say that would be of a wider help to this project. People have been pretty vocal here on numerous issues but it's awkward that people feel they have to resort to a community-maintained fork (originally for tooling compat) instead of the official threejs OrbitControls to get enhancements and bug fixes across the line.

People don't feel welcome maybe because they're often not (in the case of OrbitControls) or for reasons that aren't immediately clear to them. It's frustrating to send in a bug fix from a design issue raised multiple times until it was explicitly documented as "wontfix" for it to be shut down as intentional design -- no less, an edge case. Seems there's already a long history here. As a prerequisite to a PR, I might have looked for any prior decisions made in issues or documentation but this specifically is confusing from an outsider's perspective.

> How about updating the contribution guide? The note at the end of the documentation is already somewhat related. Maybe it can be extended?

Would this be good to have on the website? I know the contributing file already follows existing common convention, but wonder if it's worthwhile to have this searchable in the docs.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body:
Why are devs bothering with doing PRs?
Because it's better to fix the cause of an issue than to
- patch it up, 
- introduce another dependency,
- introduce duplicated code,
- introduce a fork.

None of the above helps other people, the bug is still present then, and someone else will stumble upon it, as can be seen from the many related PRs and issues here. I hope it's clear that we're trying to contribute. This is not some random product-specific addition.

@Mugen87 would you mind pointing me to the place in the Contributing doc where differences between contributing to core and examples are explained? I didn't find that upon re-reading. Or is that what you'd want to add? 
If so, would that be something like "don't expect fixes to examples being accepted, fixing them would make them less example-y", or "do not make PRs that improve the code in /examples or introduce new usecases", or "please note that PRs will only be accepted if they remove lines"? Sorry if that's tongue-in-cheek but I'd really like to understand this problem, and right now I don't.

I'm still not sure what the disagreement here is, to be honest.
1. The problem fixed by this PR is not a bug?
2. This PR is too complex?
3. This PR would introduce new problems and increase maintenance burden in the future? If so, in which way?

If the only outcome of answering the above is improving the Contribution guide so be it...

That is where many discussions ended in the past just like this one.

body:
As I was recently hit by this and tracked down the reason, not noticing the issue was fixed meanwhile, I would like to add explanation why this optimization produced wrong result.

The trouble is `setValueT1` not only sets the uniforms and bind textures, but also increments the texture unit counter, which is done in `allocateTextureUnit`. If this optimization is attempted again, care must be taken to call `allocateTextureUnit` when skipping the corresponding setter call, or the texture unit computation needs to be reworked and moved outside of `setValueT1`, which might require more substantial rework, but would seem more logical to me.

body:
I think I still prefer what you had...

For example:

```js
import { Frustum, Matrix4, Vector3, Color, LinearEncoding } from 'three';
```

It's impossible to tell what is a class and what is a constant. 

This is more readable:

```js
import { Frustum, Matrix4, Vector3, Color, LINEAR_ENCODING } from 'three';
```

But I think what you have done is even better.

For example:

```js
import { Frustum, Matrix4, Vector3, Color, Encoding } from 'three';

texture.encoding = Encoding.LINEAR;
```

So I vote for keeping the current style in the Nodes system.
And maybe, at some point, we could change the constants style in core to be more like the Nodes one.

@Mugen87 What do you think?

author: WestLangley

body:
I did give a try to this feature. It works like a charm. I was able to bake a static shadow on the whole scene in 4k and then render a small dynamic shadow in 1k that calls renderShadowMap only on a few meshes:

(In this video the giant shadow camera helper is the shadow camera of the scene, and the small shadow camera helper is the dynamic that only renders the characters through `renderShadowMap`)

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15867665/159166252-c066c3dd-53a5-42e6-82ba-2e05135f3e5d.mov

Also, to make full usage of it I would recommend a 4th parameter `forceUpdate`

Like so:

`this.renderShadowMap = function ( lightsWithShadows, scene, camera, forceUpdate )`

That will allow being able to manually force to render an object even though `autoUpdate` and `needsUpdate` are set to false. We need to disable both these values if with use renderShadowMap otherwise it would clash with the natural state of how `shadowmap.render` works and call both methods per render.

In that case, the render method of `WebGLShadowMap` would have a third condition in order to prevent the render of the shadow:
`if ( shadow.autoUpdate === false && shadow.needsUpdate === false && forceUpdate === undefined ) continue;`

body:
Hello @WestLangley, I think Im a bit late but still Im going to post the comment :)

I tested the code but it still darkens the lightmaps on meshBasicMaterials, I believe it should only be affecting when lights can affect the material (lambert above)

right now this happens to mesh basic material with defined lightmap when I turn on physically correct lights off (left) and on (right)

![img1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1117257/156254762-64669542-c25e-4391-bb87-fd5558a39282.png)

To cancel the effect I changed the line of code in meshbasic.glsl.js:
```
reflectedLight.indirectDiffuse += lightMapTexel.rgb * lightMapIntensity * RECIPROCAL_PI;
```

to: 
```
reflectedLight.indirectDiffuse += lightMapTexel.rgb * lightMapIntensity;
#ifdef PHYSICALLY_CORRECT_LIGHTS
	reflectedLight.indirectDiffuse *= PI;
#endif
```

And leave the rest of the pr as it is, this way, meshBasicMaterial lightmap intensity will always be multiplied by PI, even when physicallyCorrectLights is defined, here is the final comparison

![img2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1117257/156255077-a8b03574-21df-4389-801b-372c40f45e3c.png)

Thanks for the follow up on the topic! :)
author: memelotsqui

author: WestLangley

body:
>@WestLangley How would you formulate a note for the migration guide regarding MeshBasicMaterial?

@Mugen87 The implementation of this legacy feature is arbitrary. Let's see how this plays out. I'll keep your question in mind...
author: WestLangley

body:
For us in Hubs on r133, with `physicallyCorrectLights` always on, coming out of Blender we currently have to set our `lightmapIntensity` to PI (but this blows out the lightmap on **MeshBasicMaterial**).

This PR (and https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23197) fix the inconsistency between **MeshStandardMaterial** and **MeshBasicMaterial** which seems correct to me. As discussed previously, its a bit weird that **MeshBasicMaterial** has support for lightmaps in the first place, but since it does I would think it should be effected in the same way by `physicallyCorrectLights`.

If I am not mistaken for our usecase even with this PR (or #23197) we will still need to set our lightmap intensity to PI for the lightmaps we are getting out of Blender (and apparently Unity according to https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23197#issuecomment-1019390475) but that seems sane enough to do in our client code or three fork if needed.

If I am understanding the problem correctly, the reason we still have to do this is that what we are generating in our lightmaps is not actually the incoming light but the diffuse light reflecting off the surface (without its color https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/21912#issuecomment-854273799), so we end up with lightmaps that are already multiplied by 1/PI, these then get multiplied by 1/PI again by `BRDF_Lambert`. We set our `lightmapIntensity` to PI to cancel out this second multiplication by 1/PI. 

I do want to know what toolchain people are actually using though that results in lightmaps that will work correctly with a `lightmapIntensity` of 1. If there isn't a good path for this, it might make sense to have the default be to consider the lightmap data to already have 1/PI baked in, and document + act accordingly.

Pretty sure everything I said above is correct, but I will try and test this change with some of our assets soon and reply if anything is off. Thanks for iterating on this with us.
author: netpro2k

body:
>its a bit weird that MeshBasicMaterial has support for lightmaps in the first place

Right. It shouldn't. A light map is just per-uv ambient light. But `MeshBasicMaterial` doesn't respond to ambient light. So why does the material support light maps? (The answer is: it is a legacy thing.)

As I have said previously, what makes sense to me is to remove support for light maps from `MeshBasicMaterial`. Users can use `MeshLamberMaterial`, instead.
author: WestLangley

body:
I think I might have diverged from the main issue :O, but testing it again, it does now have consistency when going from standard material to mesh basic when multiplying meshBasic by Reciprocal_PI :), as mentioned by netpro2k lightmap was being blown out before with physicalCorrectLights turned on.


> If I am not mistaken for our usecase even with this PR (or #23197) we will still need to set our lightmap intensity to PI

Totally, I will be multiplying by PI to when physicallyCorrectLights are turned on


> I do want to know what toolchain people are actually using though that results in lightmaps that will work correctly with a `lightmapIntensity` of 1

Based on what ive seen (I might be wrong), If we want to have a lightmap working correctly with a value of 1, we would need to use HDR lightmaps instead of LDR (but thats totally not a good idea due to hdr image size). Since lightmaps are multiplied to the main color and LDR cant go above value of 1, we would only be adding shadows, if we multiply it by PI, we will actually have a maximum value of light of PI in the lightmaps :) (at least thats what I think, Im not sure if Im correct here)

> As I have said previously, what makes sense to me is to remove support for light maps from `MeshBasicMaterial`. Users can use `MeshLamberMaterial`, instead.

I think its a cool really cheap material that fakes illumination in no light scenes :), Im not sure how much difference there is in performance between meshBasic and meshLambert, but it would be nice to keep lightmaps on meshBasic :D

Thanks for the follow up and clarifications :D
author: memelotsqui

body:
> If we want to have a lightmap working correctly with a value of 1, we would need to use HDR lightmaps instead of LDR

We are actually already doing this in Hubs. They are certainly larger but the tradeoff can be made on a case by case basis by artists creating content. In many cases it is worth trading resolution for dynamic range for a given GPU memory footprint. We also use HDR environment maps, though those tend to be very low resolution.

The issue for us is the lightmaps we are generating in Blender seem to already have their values multiplied by 1/PI (just as a result of the final surface bounce in path tracing). So when using in THREE with `physicalCorrectLights` turned on they will appear darker than expected (since `BRDF_Lambert` again multiplies the value by 1/PI). To negate this second multiplication we set our lightmap intensity to PI. 

Like I said, I am fine doing this in our client code or branch, but I would still like to know what workflow we actually want people to be using to not have to work around it like this. It might be we just need to make workflow adjustments instead.

body:
So, `KHR_materials_pbrSpecularGlossiness` is still a thing?... 

See https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/pull/2078
author: WestLangley

body:
New glTF material features (like sheen, transmission, refraction, IOR, iridescence, subsurface scattering, ...) are only added to the metal/rough PBR workflow by Khronos now and moving forward, so the spec/gloss workflow in glTF provided by `KHR_materials_pbrSpecularGlossiness` is basically deprecated. 

With extensions like `KHR_materials_specular` and `KHR_materials_ior`, the metal/rough workflow can losslessly represent all of the same materials as the spec/gloss workflow anyway, and there are some tools (like glTF Transform) to convert spec/gloss to metal/rough.

I think we could consider removing support for spec/gloss.
author: donmccurdy

body: Thanks @Mugen87! Yeah, Spec-Gloss is deprecated, but only for about a year now and there are lots of assets floating around with the extension because many authoring tools didn't like the idea of a lossy conversion. I would wait until there is a high quality free tool for converting Spec-Gloss GLBs to MR before we remove support.
author: elalish

body:
Not intending to rush the process, but -

```
gltf-transform metalrough input.glb output.glb
```

The conversion is not lossy if the viewer supports the specular and IOR extensions.

body: Ah the problem is that cloned textures for render targets now have a version greater `0`. That will make `WebGLTextures` triggering a texture upload which is not valid for render targets. Fixing...
author: Mugen87

body: @Mugen87 if we have two textures with the same Source, what does `texture.needsUpdate = true` do now? In the past I've assumed that property causes a re-upload to the GPU... is that still true with a shared Source?
author: donmccurdy

body:
No, this does not happen with `Source` anymore if setting `needsUpdate` to `true` happens in the same frame for both textures.

The are a couple of design issues in `Texture` that prevent the implementation from avoiding any possible overhead. E.g. to make the implementation backwards compatible, setting `Texture.needsUpdate` also triggers `Source.needsUpdate`. Besides, properties like `Texture.type` and `Texture.format` which are closely coupled to the data are still part of `Texture`. So depending on how you configure an instance of `Texture`, there might be additional GPU uploads. But not in the "standard" case.

author: mrdoob

body:
As far as I can see, this is not the only change to make `OrbitControls` work in a worker. Read the following guide for more details:

https://threejs.org/manual/?q=worker#en/offscreencanvas

I don't think we should start adding worker support for controls. It might be better to keep the controls in the main thread (with a dummy object) and just report the updated transformation to the worker thread.
author: Mugen87

body:
> As far as I can see, this is not the only change to make `OrbitControls` work in a worker. Read the following guide for more details:
> 
> https://threejs.org/manual/?q=worker#en/offscreencanvas
> 
> I don't think we should start adding worker support for controls. It might be better to keep the controls in the main thread (with a dummy object) and just report the updated transformation to the worker thread.

I examined the example and it is pretty much what we do in our case. The example, as well as we do serialize dom events  and send them to the web worker and the web worker runs the OrbitControls along with the camera. If you want to discourage this kind of usage, I would recommend changing the example.

Moreover I think it is more elegant to make the OrbitControls use and interface, combining EventDispatcher with some additional props instead of a dom element. Then you can decide where it should live.

In our case, this comes in handy:
1. We have multiple canvases running with the same OrbitControls and camera. Having it centrelized in the worker does not need synchronization with multiple instances in the main thread. The whole three js scene lives in the workers.
2. We do not have any three.js dependencies bundled in our main bundle. Its all in the 3d worker bundle. This makes our main bundle smaller.

But I found an alternate solution which makes this merge request obsolete. You can set: "self.document" to "undefined" (it is also done in the example). Then document is visible in the global scope and threejs OrbitControls won't crash in the web worker. But sounds like a bit hacky to me. Maybe it is also an solution to check for self.document instead of document in OrbitControls and we are always fine. No hack needed. What do you think?
author: begmec

body:
> But I found an alternate solution which makes this merge request obsolete. You can set: "self.document" to "undefined" (it is also done in the example). Then document is visible in the global scope and threejs OrbitControls won't crash in the web worker.

Let's use this approach for now and don't start modifying the controls. I'm afraid making the controls "worker-ready" will introduce a lot of complexity since you will need some sort of abstraction instead of working directly with the DOM. That is somewhat out of scope right now.

body:
Just wanted to reference this forum discussion here regarding the "perceived smoothness" of the background when using regular mipmaps vs. when using the mipmaps from PMREM. I've often seen game engines actually use the latter because it leads to smoother appearance (less jagged/pixelated) at medium desired blurriness values. At full blurriness=1 it doesn't make a difference.
https://discourse.threejs.org/t/ability-to-reuse-the-pmrem-generated-texture-from-scene-environment/37752
author: hybridherbst

body: BTW: If we decide to use PMREM for background blurriness, we can use (parts of) #20463. I've started to use PMREM since BabylonJS does it the same way.
author: Mugen87

body: Seems #20463 has landed (as part of #22178) - think it would make sense to use PMREM for background blurriness then and continue here?
author: hybridherbst

body: I would be fine with that. However, @WestLangley had objections against the PMREM solution (see https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/20115#issuecomment-675644813). I would only rewrite the PR if he agrees on PMREM, too.
author: Mugen87

body:
I see. Copying in the images from my post above fo quicker comparison here.

| Blurriness with just mipmaps | Blurriness with PMREM |
|-|-|
| ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2693840/193584153-78399d2a-f32b-4d83-b4ac-6d8ee5a0974e.png) |![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2693840/193584213-41957e38-0bcf-4921-ada2-f4ca8b93b4bf.png) |


My opinion would be that neither are perfect (would need to sample more times to get perfectly smooth results), but PMREM gets closer to the desired goal of "smooth blur" while just mipmaps leave non-smooth jagged edges.

body:
> Such partial definitions are problematic for logic operating on group data.

That's a surprise to me - I'd assumed groups could be resized smaller (like `.drawRange`) safely. Are you referring to renderer logic, exporter logic, or runtime operations like bounding boxes and raycasting?

For another example, is it OK if all indices are used but they only point to only a subset of the vertices? This may need to be explained in a bit more detail.
author: donmccurdy

body: I've planned to add a helper for converting a multi-material mesh into several meshes. This feature requires merging of groups for a proper implementation. Not having the above policy makes an implementation noticeably more complex. Hence, I'd like to have a more strict usage of `groups` and avoid unnecessary complexity.
author: Mugen87

body:
> For another example, is it OK if all indices are used but they only point to only a subset of the vertices? 

What is the use case of such a setup? TBH, I do not want to honor use cases which are theoretically possible just because the API currently allows it but are not relevant in production. There should always be a compelling reasons why edges cases should be supported.
author: Mugen87

body:
> What is the use case of such a setup? TBH, I do not want to honor use cases which are theoretically possible ...

This is a very real use case, at least for geometry that does _not_ use groups. For example, I might define a large vertex buffer, and then have several meshes drawn with different materials, each using indices that point to a subset of the vertex buffer. Is that a problem for the helper you want to add? Or only a problem if the mesh has ≥2 groups? GLTFLoader can create meshes like this, but never creates groups.

Personally I'm lukewarm on multi-material meshes and `geometry.groups`. I feel like they might be more maintenance burden than they're worth, and don't pair well with APIs like InstancedMesh or BatchedMesh. I certainly don't feel like grouped geometry needs to be supported in every API.
author: donmccurdy

body:
> Is that a problem for the helper you want to add?

No it isn't. As long as the groups cover the entire index (so there are no gaps).

`WebGPURenderer` does not support groups/multi materials and there is no plan so far to add this feature. The helper should make it possible to convert multi-material meshes to a representation that can be handled by both renderers. It is also useful for exporters, see #22555.

Eventually, I hope we can stop multi-material support in `WebGLRenderer` at some point. The helper is then required in any case.
author: Mugen87

body:
I wonder if it would be worthwhile to log some kind of warning when the renderer detects a geometry that doesn't meet this requirement. That might help us to discover any use cases we've missed before enforcing this rule. I can't think of use cases myself but it would surprise me if there weren't a few...

In any case, I'm comfortable with this PR. 👍
author: donmccurdy

body:
> I wonder if it would be worthwhile to log some kind of warning when the renderer detects a geometry that doesn't meet this requirement.

I'm afraid it will be a bit problematic to detect such geometries since a reliable check needs to be done per frame. And the check itself is not that trivial. Right now, I recommend to not enhance the renderer with validation checks and wait how many users actually complain about the upcoming `BufferGeometryUtils.mergeGroups()` and `SceneUtils.createMeshesFromMultiMaterialMesh()`.

author: mrdoob

body:
> Do you mind elaborating what's the intent here?

Hello @mrdoob. Well my intent is to secure the script execution. eg: if somenone shares a JSON of a project, you could be importing evil code ( use of eval(), access to the cookies, access to current document... ). So I felt like it would be a good thing to add an iframe to run the scripts just like JSFiddle does, in case someone's imports some untrusted code into the editor. Hope's it makes things clearer for you. Thanks in advance.
EDIT: adding/removing iframe could be moved outside of the `for` loop.
author: JSmithOner

body:
Hi @JSmithOner! Thanks for looking into this and opening a PR.

Unfortunately the security model for a site like JSFiddle is more complex. Without getting into [sandboxing](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/iframe#attr-sandbox) too much, the iframe needs to be cut off from the host page and ideally running on a different subdomain. This prevents one user's JSFiddle script from accessing another user's JSFiddle account.

In the three.js editor the main defense is simply not having any user accounts or auth-protected resources in the first place. So there's very little incentive to try XSS or other attacks, and little to be gained if one succeeds. I think that's probably sufficient for the purposes of the editor.

Reference: https://security.stackexchange.com/questions/254512/how-do-i-safely-host-third-party-javascript-code-in-an-iframe
author: donmccurdy

body: @donmccurdy Thank you for your feedback, yes I've noticed their wasn't any critical cookies that could be retrieved ( auth, session ), but I felt like this would be better not being able to run bad eval or accessing document, this prevent having bad surprises running untrusted source code even if it's ( I repeat myself ) not critical. For the stack overflow post I 'll read this in detail later as I am building an editor myself ( Thanks again ).
author: JSmithOner

body:
To secure user scripts, it's necessary to understand what the scripts should and should not be able to do. Moving the script into an iframe does not make untrusted code safe by itself — code in an iframe can still access `parent.document` unless further safety measures are taken. 

Moreover, I'm not sure we want to remove access to the document – I haven't used scripts in the editor much myself but I think they're supposed to be able to modify the scene, which won't be available under a secure iframe with sandboxing.

I'd recommend reading the stack overflow post above and perhaps more about [user content subdomains](https://security.stackexchange.com/questions/23220/user-specific-subdomains-javascript-security) if you're interested in building something like this yourself, but I'm afraid it is probably not practical or necessary for https://threejs.org/editor/ at this time.

body:
Hey, the main use case I have for this is the ability to blend by a specific alpha while preserving the alpha of the source.

Basically I can set 
- blendDst = ConstantAlpha
- blendDstAlpha = ZeroFactor
- blendSrc = OneMinusConstantAlpha
- blendSrcAlpha = OneFactor

Then let blendColor be (0.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.5) f.ex.

This is extremely useful because you can use the alpha channel to store other data than transparency. In this case I can blend the colors by the constant alpha, but the actual alpha value of the source will be preserved, which is necessary if you encode some specific byte data into the alpha channel that you want to use at a later stage in your rendering pipeline. Basically, if you have a constant alpha blending, you can free that channel for other usages by using these blend factors.



Does this use-case make sense? I'll fix the example (was working fine for me locally, but seems I messed something up as it clearly isn't working as intended in the link you shared). Also I will fix a proper default value of (0, 0, 0, 0).
author: christjt

body: There also are WebGPU's [BlendColorFactor and OneMinusBlendColorFactor](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/examples/jsm/renderers/webgpu/constants.js#L258-L261). They should also possibly be added.
author: LeviPesin

author: WestLangley

body:
> > Does this use-case make sense?
> > ...but seems I messed something up as it clearly isn't working as intended in the link you shared
> 
> Your math, as you described it, does not seem to me to be consistent with your objective.

I am probably explaining this very poorly. This feature enables users to blend two different objects by a color and/or alpha that is not defined by the objects themselves. 

I'll try to contrive a better example:

Imagine you have some player object that is walking either in-front or behind some wall. When the player walks in-front of the wall you want everything to be rendered opaque, but when the player is behind the wall you want a slight blend such that the player kan be slightly seen through the wall (like some sort of ghost effect or something).

To achieve this effect without drawing the player in two passes. You can draw the player first (writing to the depth buffer), and then draw the wall (with depth test) while blending it by some constant color that you wish for your effect (maybe some red color or something).

Even if we disregard the performance cost of having to draw the player twice (once with in front color and once with behind color), it would be cumbersome for partially visible player (at the edge of a corner or something) because you could end up where the two players-renders are depth fighting. You could perhaps get away with using a stencil and some other tricks. But in the end you are still paying for two passes when it could be achieved with one.

You see this type of visualization often in shooter games where they have some visualization where you can see players behind walls (often getting some color tint applied).

Here is an example where you can see the players behind a wall, but tinted red only when behind.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6595726/161294599-bc149d9e-9fb9-49ea-8b2c-3654731035f9.png)


 Btw. I am not trying to create a wall-hack visualization. Just looking to implement the effect :P

body:
That looks indeed cleaner however I'm not sure multi-materials or a similar feature like `Compound` is required at all. It's actually easier to enable/use stuff like batching or instanced rendering if no such logic exists. Besides, the engine provides an existing way to achieve more or less the same in a different way (by using multiple instances of `BufferGeometry` and a combination of `Group`/`Meshes`).

`WebGPURenderer` does currently not support multi-materials and if there will be no compelling demand from user side, I think it's better to stop multi-material support and provide no substitute.
author: Mugen87

body:
I think something like `SubBufferGeometry` is more intuitive and more flexible. With `.groups` you need to make sure that the index of the array matches the index in the multi-material array. The `SubBufferGeometry` approach is less error prone in that regard.

It also makes it much easier  to render different parts of the same geometry data in different parts of the scene. With groups is much more convoluted to do that.

Hmm... `BufferGeometryView` may be a better name indeed.

The `Compound` class is to keep the current behavior in which groups do not have transforms. But also, I think it matches better with GLTF because you can have different meshes in the same node/transform.

@donmccurdy What do you think?
author: mrdoob

body:
Hmm... This approach would also make multi-material obsolete, which is also code/design I would love to get rid of...

The only use case I can think of where this makes things more difficult are when dealing with `SkinnedMesh` with groups.
author: mrdoob

body: MMD might be a good example to check if the functionalities keep working because it consists of groups, SkinnedMesh, morph, and keyframe animations.
author: takahirox

body:
### multi-material / `geometry.groups` / `Compound`

Removing multi-material and `geometry.groups` would be a good direction, I think. What makes things more difficult with `SkinnedMesh`, though?

I'm not sure I see the need for a `Compound` class that ignores transforms, but I'm not opposed to it. Perhaps it should not allow >1 layer of nesting? Or what would happen if you put an Object3D or Group into a Compound? Another idea for a name would be something like `TransformGroup` to imply it's similar to `Group` and that all objects share the same transform.

***

### `BufferGeometryView` / `SubBufferGeometry` 

If we introduce an API like `BufferGeometryView` / `SubBufferGeometry`  I hope we could plan how they might work with (or replace) the proposals below:

- https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/17089
- https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/21656

More concretely, do we want an API allowing multiple `Mesh` instances to use different parts of a `BufferGeometry`, or of a WebGL buffer? The latter is more low-level, but potentially allows more optimization too. 

Perhaps it's too different but I'll bring up the BatchedMesh proposal as well ...

- https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/22376

... this isn't a direct parallel for BufferGeometryView of course, but it is another way to draw many meshes from a single geometry.

author: swift502

body:
@swift502 
I'm afraid I don't have much to contribute to this discussion. I wrote this code back in 2019 and have little time nowadays to maintain / investigate this. There's of course others who are far more invested than I am in perfecting FXAA and I'll happily leave it to them to do so.
What I can provide is context on the changes I had made to the Nvidia shader. There were a few major issues that were relevant to us when we tried the Nvidia shader in production and thus the goal of my changes was specifically to fix the following:
- It did not support transparency
- It compared pixels based on Luminance, thus some edges were entirely missed by the shader
- It didn't handle edges well unless they were near 45 degrees. ~45deg edges are easy to handle because you only need to tap a few adjacent pixels and take the average of their color. I implemented a pixel-march that walked along a horizontal/vertical edge until it no longer detected the edge (due to it shifting 1 pixel perpendicularly), then used that distance to determine the % by which the pixels on each side of the edge should be mixed.
author: DanielSturk

body:
@DanielSturk Thanks a lot for the response. I guess my other concern is if it's still fair to call your algorithm FXAA?

1. You mention Luminance was not doing a good job, but it is mentioned frequently in the FXAA white paper, does your new algorithm completely replace that?

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/12886219-905d-45a2-a1e3-20ab65979d0c)

2. Every other implementation I've seen seems to be using these lookup tables, but you also seemed to have abandoned that?

```glsl
    #define FXAA_QUALITY_P0 1.0
    #define FXAA_QUALITY_P1 1.5
    #define FXAA_QUALITY_P2 2.0
    #define FXAA_QUALITY_P3 4.0
    #define FXAA_QUALITY_P4 12.0
```

If your algorithm has significant changes from "traditional" FXAA implementations, wouldn't it be more fitting to offer it as another AA method, alongside FXAA and it's flaws?
author: swift502

body:
@swift502
1. I replaced the luminance comparison with the simple `contrast()` function I wrote
2. I replaced that quality parameter with `NUM_SAMPLES`, an `int` representing the # of steps to take

I have no opinion on the naming. To clarify, this wasn't an in-depth research project but rather a 2-3 day effort to fix the limitations with the shader that were blocking our company at the time. Afterwards, BenHouston contributed these changes back to mainline in this PR

For context, I made this PR because to simply look at a glTF file (on release or on the dev branch) (to check https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23764) there's a cumbersome number of steps needed, as the editor comes with its own set of issues, bugs, and complexity. body: The problem is the definition "minimal example". What does that entail? Eventually, we can quickly end up with all basic features required for a PBR viewer. Meaning drag'n'drop, IBL setup, animation playback, automatic camera adjustment, support for compressed assets (KTX/DRACO) and so on. This gets quickly too much. Hence, I vote to not add a viewer example. Don's viewer is compact enough so it's appropriate as a template for a basic viewer implementation.
author: Mugen87

body:
Thanks for the clarification! I kinda disagree that a 750 line file is a good basic example if all that's needed to bring "here's a single model" to a basic "you can drop your own" viewer are like 10 lines. If you can find your way around in that that's great but then you don't need an example anymore anyways.

Follow-up question would be, already a good case for "why would a file loading example in three be good to have": what's the right way to track the meshes/materials/textures loaded via the file? 
(1) Traverse meshes/materials, iterate all material keys and check if they are textures?
(2) I looked at LoadingManager, should one manually keep track of assets that were loaded there?

The [implementation in Don's viewer](https://github.com/donmccurdy/three-gltf-viewer/blob/2784c12c4917eda6a97e0b101951a5a47c066bc6/src/viewer.js#L704) has a hardcoded list of textures to dispose which will for example not track if textures are loaded as part of extensions (e.g. volume/transmission textures would already not be tracked there).

**EDIT:** I implemented (1) and adjusted the drag-drop behaviour to match the MatCap sample. Happy to split up into a separate example once a conclusion is reached here :)

author: WestLangley

body:
Honestly the web's drag-and-drop [dataTransfer](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/DataTransfer) API is ... challenging. To properly support folders in any real project, I would have a look at small helper libraries like [simple-dropzone](https://github.com/donmccurdy/simple-dropzone) or [flat-drop-files](https://github.com/placemark/flat-drop-files) — both include more workarounds than probably belong in a three.js example.

Perhaps just supporting `.glb` would simplify this enough to be more practical? We could include an explanatory comment pointing to these libraries for users who want to support folders, ZIPs, large directories, etc.

author: takahirox

body:
I've fixed the title, adjusted the HDRI, adjusted min-max logic, and refactored animation playback to ensure it works for both glTF and FBX.
The reason for adding both was to have an "loader_multiple" example that shows how to deal with the differences and similarities in loaders and scene structures.

I've also added background bluriness based on https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23712; in the commit history is also a version that uses PMREM instead of simple mipmaps but that's a different discussion.

Having this example has already made working on three.js a lot easier for me, as there's now a nice basic example to add more advanced viewer features on top, or to test out glTF extensions, post effects on different models, ...
author: hybridherbst

body:
Rebased on dev. I still find this incredibly useful to 
a) have a reference for implementing drag-drop loaders
b) a quick way to check what three supports / doesn't support when it comes to glTF (and FBX and potentially other formats).
author: hybridherbst

body: Rebased on latest dev, and replaced the background blurriness workaround with `backgroundBlurriness`.
author: hybridherbst

body:
Rebased to latest three. 

I can also close this PR if there's no interest in a more complete "here's how to build a viewer" sample.
It has been super valuable for me to have this example while getting started with three.js and contributing to it.
I keep referencing it a lot, and test most of PRs related to Loaders/Exporters in three.js with this sample.

author: sunag

body:
I am not sure that I understand what "does not bind NodeMaterial dependencies in the core" means...

The main problem with `NodeMaterial.fromMaterial` is the circular dependency problem which cannot be solved, because classes are initialized synchronously (and there is no way to change this).
I.e. this means if I write `import MeshBasicNodeMaterial from './MeshBasicNodeMaterial.js'; export default class NodeMaterial {}` in NodeMaterial.js and `import NodeMaterial from './NodeMaterial.js'; export default class MeshBasicNodeMaterial extends NodeMaterial {}` in MeshBasicNodeMaterial.js, it will break (with something like "NodeMaterial is not initialized", I think this depends on the browser).

body:
`BypassNode` can be used mainly to call `void` functions in the flow process. If we remove this, assume we will not have support to any `FunctionNode` that returns `void`.

It is possible to notice in two Skinning examples that `BypassNode` is used for flow control, and not necessarly for a simple assigning.
author: sunag

body: @LeviPesin Still [this fix](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23797#issuecomment-1082734924) break Skinning Points. It is safer to introduce the BypassNode to leave the assigns in the final process. I think that it can be only `bypass( target, voidNode )`.
author: sunag

body:
> It is possible to notice in two Skinning examples that BypassNode is used for flow control, and not necessarly for a simple assigning.

But in SkinningNode it can be fixed changing `SkinningNode extends Node` to `SkinningNode extends TempNode` and `BypassNode( vertex, SkinningNode )` to just `SkinningNode` (because as I understand TempNode and BypassNode `BypassNode( Node, TempNode ).build()` is the same as `TempNode.build(); Node.build()`).

author: LeviPesin

body:
> @sunag So what do you think about WebGLNodeBuilder? Is there a way for it to use the NodeMaterial.fromMaterial().build() so that it will not break the lighting (i.e. how to construct the lightNode)?

In case of the user use the property `.lightNode` it can added extra lights results between `lights_fragment_begin` and `lights_fragment_end`. I think that it is the first step.

We still don't have some optimizers for `WebGL` for this reason some calcs can be duplicated like `normals` used in lights and skinning too. This seems complicated at the moment.
author: sunag

body:
> In case of the user use the property .lightNode it can added extra lights results between lights_fragment_begin and lights_fragment_end. I think that it is the first step.

I meant the scene lightNode (and fogNode), similarly to how it is done in WebGPUNodeBuilder. If we add some code to construct them, then we can remove a large part of the WebGLNodeBuilder (that allows using "native" shaders) and replace it with just `NodeMaterial.fromMaterial().build()`, the same as it was done by me for WebGPUNodeBuilder.

> We still don't have some optimizers for WebGL for this reason some calcs can be duplicated like normals used in lights and skinning too. This seems complicated at the moment.

I am not sure that even WebGPUNodeBuilder's current optimizers are working too good... See https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23728.

body:
@gsimone I initially thought about making the provider be a member of the class, but then reconsidered it not to introduce new class members and keep it closer to original and just determine it once instead of doing so every time instance created. I just didn't expect it would break the tests O_O 

So taking into consideration that both types of implementations yield almost identical performance, making it a class member is indeed favorable :) (maybe even getting rid of `now` method and just use `this.provider.now()` ?)

Also I've done some more investigation and testing regarding the performance and the results are quite interesting.
Most of the time Class/Module are slightly faster than Original, but for some reason on Chrome (latest ver., Windows 10) Original is ~5x times slower, on Chrome Android ~1.6x times slower

author: WestLangley

body:
Oh tbh I didn't realize that `BufferGeometry.computeTangents` had been implemented. I do see that GLTF recommends MikkTSpace normals if no tangents are provided in a model, though.

> When tangents are not specified, client implementations SHOULD calculate tangents using default [MikkTSpace](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/blob/main/specification/2.0/Specification.adoc#mikktspace) algorithms with the specified vertex positions, normals, and texture coordinates associated with the normal texture.

But here's an image with the different compute tangent methods. Notice on the BufferGeometry.computeTangents that the normal map seems inverted -- this is likely due to the `negateSign` argument that is included in the MikkT function in this PR. Perhaps a negate argument could be added to the BufferGeometry version? Perhaps the built in version would be good enough in most cases, then. I'll have to look at the tangents helper another time, though.

| MikkT | computeTangents |
|---|---|
| ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/734200/160445020-df49d708-7ccc-4b23-9df9-ba8156f08f84.png) | ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/734200/160444935-1cb54bda-b08a-4c3d-ba7e-ff7ab6fd5461.png) |

@gernotziegler can make this easier with last week's gltf-transform release:

```bash
npm install --global @gltf-transform/cli

# Optimize .jpeg textures
gltf-transform mozjpeg input.glb output.glb

# Optimize .png textures
gltf-transform oxipng input.glb output.glb

# Convert all textures to .webp
gltf-transform webp input.glb output.glb

# Convert specific textures to .webp
gltf-transform webp input.glb output.glb --slots "baseColor*"
```

I would agree the download sizes tend to be better with WebP, and browser support is now pretty good. Once you start having a lot of textures — or needing to load new textures without freezing the page — KTX2 compression may be a better tradeoff (despite more JPEG-like sizes), given the memory and performance benefits. Or you could mix/match, e.g. using WebP for the normal maps and KTX2 for base color.
author: donmccurdy

body:
@donmccurdy :
Thanks! I have used gltf-transform before (to convert some KTX2) - thank you for this tool!  

But I thought that WebP was required-usage in GLTFs generated by gltf-transform?
https://gltf-transform.donmccurdy.com/classes/extensions.texturewebp.html

"When the EXT_texture_webp extension is added to a file by glTF-Transform, the extension should always be required. This tool does not support writing assets that "fall back" to optional PNG or JPEG image data."
Is that still the case?  Because for my current projects, I was still aiming for a JPEG fallback (large audience for museum). Maybe it is more common to implement this fallback outside the GLTF loader. 


I have understood that there is a trade-off between transmission size (Webp) and GPU memory needs (KTX2 better). In our case, transmission size was more important; therefore we used a bumpmap instead of a normal map and side-load it outside the GLTF of the 3D model. (Question: Is there a reason why they havent been standardised for GLTF?)
author: gernotziegler

body:
> But I thought that WebP was required-usage in GLTFs generated by gltf-transform?

That's true, it converts the files and doesn't leave JPEG or PNG images behind as fallbacks. Personally I think I prefer hosting alternate versions of the whole model, but your approach sounds good too.

Bump maps have been discussed a bit (e.g. see https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/issues/948). I think I tend to see bump maps as more of a burden to require on client/engine implementations than the transmission size would justify. Something like RG normal maps could be a possibility.
author: donmccurdy

body:
@donmccurdy 
I hear you :-) but I don´t think that bump maps are much of a burden for three.js (that actually has bump map functionality built-in). And from a GPU perspective, I see it actually as an advantage that bump maps only need to be single channel versus triple channels for normal maps (or double channel if you reconstruct the third channel) - this is because nowadays, GPU shader run-times suffer more from bandwidth limits than compute limits during rendering.   

I also made a comparison of bump map vs. normal map for good measure, using the model data for https://www.geofront.eu/demos/faust (double-click to open the book). For 8192x8192, the bump map requires 9MB, while the normal map takes 21 MB. That makes a difference in user patience. 

But this all just a personal opinion, no intention to convince you - we will create a custom GLTF extension eventually, anyways, if Khronos doesn´t want to standardize it for the moment. Just haven´t had the time to modify the Blender exporter plus three.js´ GLTFLoader. Will let you all know if something happens on this front :-)
author: gernotziegler

body: Yeah, I don't see bump maps as a particular burden in three.js. I think it was more concerns about Khronos requiring all current and future glTF implementations to support both bump and normal maps. I believe normal maps are also just more consistent – you can specify [MikkTSpace](www.mikktspace.com/) tangents and be sure that all software will display it exactly the same, whereas bump maps are going to vary more across implementations. But I understand that's all pretty theoretical and 9MB vs 21MB is a real difference in user experience — point taken! :)

> What do you think about adding three-gpu-pathtracer and three-mesh-bvh examples in this repo like with ifc.js?

I've thought about this but I think there's an extra burden. I'd prefer to not have to update the three.js repo with new builds and enjoy being able to release / fix bugs on my own cadence. Making sure that the build isn't broken in time before a three.js release after is also a lot of additional overhead to deal with. I also feel it's good to funnel people to the core project so discussions, issues, and requests can happen in the right place.

The discoverability would be good, though. With import maps being used now it's much more viable to create examples using libraries without a copy of the library being maintained in this project. Ie we could have a path tracer or bvh example that imports the libs from unpkg or skypack. It would also alleviate license compatibility issues - which the two projects in question don't have but some of my JPL open source work does (they are licensed as Apache 2.0 not MIT). And perhaps these could be a separate listing on the examples page.

Maybe it's a different can of worms in it's own way but something to consider to support the growth of external projects from the core library.

author: JordyvanDortmont

body:
tl;dr — this change looks good to me!

***

Summarizing legacy vs. physical attenuation:

- **legacy:** light attenuates from 100% to 0% linearly (`decay=1`) or by inverse square (`decay=2`) with the _ratio_ of object distance / `light.distance`, reaching 0% at `light.distance` meters. Because the range of the light is specified, and attenuation is distributed over that entire range, attenuation is not realistically based on physical distance. If either `decay=0` or `distance=0`, light does not attenuate. Distance is 0 by default, so light does not attenuate by default.
- **physical:** light attenuates from 100% to 0% linearly (`decay=1`) or by inverse square law (`decay=2`) with the _object distance_, never reaching exactly 0% unless `light.distance` is specified. When `light.distance` is specified, it smoothly but quickly diminishes intensity to zero at that distance<sup>1</sup>, without affecting attenuation over the rest of the light's range. If `decay=0`, light does not attenuate. 

_<sup>1</sup> This is not physically correct, but is a useful tool to limit the otherwise infinite range of the light to something manageable for rendering performance in a scene with many lights. Unlike in the legacy mode, `light.distance` in physical mode has very minimal effect on the physical-correctness of attenuation _within_ the range of the light._

***

In the legacy mode, attenuation is disabled by default and so the change to from `decay=1` to `decay=2` does not affect things much. Even with a distance, the light still has the same range, but at 50% of that range the light will now be at 25% strength rather than 50% strength.

In physical mode, attenuation is enabled by default, and the change in decay will more directly affect the perceived range of the light. This is not a dramatic difference but it is visible. 

Given that most users are probably still using the legacy mode, this seems like as good a time as any to make the change. I've created a spreadsheet, below, to illustrate the changes. I approximated the quadratic dropoff at `light.distance` in physical mode as a hard cutoff for simplicity.

![light attenuation](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1848368/163514217-6d0fbdbd-5abd-460a-a034-ade377bbddf9.png)

_Source: [Punctual light attenuation in three.js](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1O1DyZwajQQzqZOEP5qWKnoxxuIFFKDaE4QKCqRVReEg/edit?usp=sharing)_

NOTE: I've omitted it from the table, but increasing intensity by a factor of Math.PI is generally useful when switching from legacy to physical mode for the first time.
author: donmccurdy

author: bhouston

body:
In physical lighting mode, the decay should be _hardwired_ to 2, otherwise it is not physically-correct.

How to handle artist-friendly mode (the current default), is up for debate.
author: WestLangley

body: The original "physical lighting mode" didn't actually force people to be physical correct, rather it just changed the defaults and equations to be physically correct unless you changed things no?  I think we just want the default to be physically correct while still allowing the user to move away to more stylized choices if they really want to.  Thus I advocate changing all default behaviour to be physically correct and removing that mode.
author: bhouston

body:
I like the approach @bhouston describes.

Setting `decay=2` as the default is a good change that can be made now, with or without further changes.

If/when we want to simplify further, we'll set `physicallyCorrectLights=true` as the default, and later remove the property entirely.

After that, if users want lights that decay in a non-realistic way, they can do so by increasing or decreasing `.decay`, with the understanding that `.decay=2` (default) is the physically-correct value. I hope that's a reasonable accommodation that allows some flexibility to deal with artistic goals, performance constraints, etc.

author: linbingquan

body:
So, there are two problems with the circular inclusion:

1) "Top-level problem". If a file B imports something from file A, while file A imports e.g. a class `B` from B, A must not use `B` on the top-level. It can be solved by changing `nodeImmutable`s of some classes (`ReflectNode`, `CubeTextureNode`, etc) to `nodeProxy`ies (but then `reflectVector()` and `reflectCube()` must be used instead of `reflectVector` and `reflectCube`, which is not very good), but...
2) ...it will not solve the problem because of the "non-lazy evaluations top-level problem". Because JavaScript does not support lazy evaluations in any form, if we write `nodeProxy( ReflectNode )`, it will try to evaluate `ReflectNode` before executing the functions, again running into the top-level problem. The only solution to this is to inline the `nodeProxy` code (or use `eval`).

Obviously these problems can be circumvented by creating one more ShaderNode file containing e.g. core, math, and basic accessors... But it is not a very reliable solution (I can easily imagine a node importing a node importing a node, so on, all of which should be exported by the ShaderNode).

body:
> I'll just not here as I have in other issues that naming this field something like canvasColorSpace could be more clear.

I think it's best if @mrdoob makes a final decision here 😇 . The options are:

- The current implemented `WebGLRenderer.outputColorSpace` (the original suggestion of #23614)
- The simplified version `WebGLRenderer.colorSpace`. However, it was highlighted in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23614#issuecomment-1107467806 that this version is confusing since the name does not distinct between working and output color space.
- @gkjohnson suggested `WebGLRenderer.canvasColorSpace`. This name would highlight `WebGLRenderer.canvasColorSpace` only affects the rendering to the canvas/default framebuffer.

After some more thinking I guess I vote for `WebGLRenderer.canvasColorSpace`. It seems indeed the most accurate/descriptive name.  I'm making this vote under the assumption that we never evaluate `WebGLRenderer.canvasColorSpace` when rendering to render targets (see https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23614#issuecomment-1107638945).
author: Mugen87

body:
Either `outputColorSpace` or `canvasColorSpace` are OK with me. The thing that confused me about the original name was that it is ignored if writing to canvas comes at the end of a post-processing chain. I wish we could fix that but I suppose it's out of scope here, and it's the behavior not the name that should ideally be changed for that issue.

I would consider parallelism among renderers though. Does `canvasColorSpace` still make sense for WebGPURenderer? With WebXR APIs? I've been hoping to add an `outputColorSpace` option to SVGRenderer at some point — the only renderer that could actually support a wider-gamut Display P3 output to display today — and obviously `canvasColorSpace` does not make sense there.
author: donmccurdy

body: Could then it be named `displayColorSpace`?
author: LeviPesin

body:
> that it is ignored if writing to canvas comes at the end of a post-processing chain

`WebGLRenderer.outputEncoding` triggers _the color space conversion in GLSL shaders_. That only works for built-in materials but not for custom shader passes. That's the reasons why you need an additional post processing pass for color space conversion.

I've tried to eliminate this inconsistency in #23019 because it also introduces a transparency issue. The idea was to remove the inline tone mapping and color space conversion and _always_ apply them with an additional post processing pass. However, this approach introduces a noticeable performance degradation. So at least with `WebGLRenderer`, we have to stick to the current approach.

> Does canvasColorSpace still make sense for WebGPURenderer?

Yes. But ideally it is not necessary anymore to perform the final color space conversion inline, see https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23019#issuecomment-1011221811.

> With WebXR APIs?

`WebXRManager` evaluates `WebGLRenderer.outputEncoding` for defining the right color space for its projection layers/framebuffers. But I think the name `canvasColorSpace` is okay in this context.

> I've been hoping to add an outputColorSpace option to SVGRenderer at some point

Well yes, in this case `outputColorSpace` would be better since it's the more generic term.

> Could then it be named displayColorSpace?

See https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23614#issuecomment-1107467806. If we decide against `canvasColorSpace`, `outputColorSpace` seems the best choice.
author: Mugen87

body:
The only part of "outputEncoding" I would change is "Encoding". Output is a common enough term for what it does, I think we can clear up any confusion around render targets in other ways.

Display would probably be too specific, as we also use the renderer with OffscreenCanvas, or to bake IBL, or to save a scene screenshot to disk.

author: donmccurdy

body:
Well, I'm gonna be pesky about naming stuff, hope you folks don't mind it.

Turns out, color-space is a loaded term. However it does have an established meaning, which is a combination of both a standard gamut and a set of transfer functions. ( and a white-point of calibration, if you wanna be really pesky about it )

But that's not what we are really changing when we set an output encode for the final render, be it in built-in shaders or post-processing step. If I'm not mistaken, THREE.js hasn't adopted any support in regards to operating in anything other than standard sRGB gamut. ( can't use Adobe RGB, DCI-P3 ... )

So, if we are not changing gamuts. The only other thing we can be doing is changing if the data is encoded as linear or perceptual data. This is sometimes referred to as EOTF ( [Electro-Optical Transfer Function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transfer_functions_in_imaging#:~:text=The%20electro%2Doptical%20transfer%20function,done%20within%20the%20display%20device) ). Which is indeed sometimes referred to as gamma or simply [sRGB](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SRGB#sRGB_definition), but in practice is just a single set of linear <-> perceptual functions possible.

I don't expect we to name it `outputEOTF` as there is no value in naming it something the large comunity won't recognize. But the word 'encode' does better represent what we are doing. Either we are rendering in linear ( energy intensity ) which can be later operated on by other various post-processing steps or perhaps exported to a file ( HDR, EXR ), or perceptually encoded which is meant to be the encoded in the way our eyes interpret light. or as I like to say  "human encoded light".

Perhaps we can keep `outputEncoding` ? 🙏
author: sciecode

body:
> If I'm not mistaken, THREE.js hasn't adopted any support in regards to operating in anything other than standard sRGB gamut.

Has not yet, but AFAIK aim to in the future.
author: LeviPesin

body:
> > If I'm not mistaken, THREE.js hasn't adopted any support in regards to operating in anything other than standard sRGB gamut.
